supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the Complaint, Moderna, Inc. is a biotechnology company that discovers, develops, and commercializes messenger RNA (“mRNA”) therapeutics and vaccines for the treatment of infectious diseases, immuno-oncology, rare diseases, autoimmune, and cardiovascular diseases in the U.S., Europe, and internationally. The Company’s products include, inter alia, mRESVIA (mRNA-1345), an mRNA respiratory syncytial virus (“RSV”) vaccine, intended to protect adults aged 60 years and older from lower respiratory tract disease caused by RSV infection.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements regarding the Company's business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) mRNA-1345 was less effective than Defendants had led investors to believe; (ii) accordingly, mRNA-1345's clinical and/or commercial prospects were overstated; and (iii) as a result, the Company's public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.
On May 28, 2025, the Court issued an Order appointing Lead Plaintiff. On June 10, the Court approved the Lead Plaintiff's selection of Lead Counsel.
supabase_cases.district_court: U.S. District Court for the District of Massachusetts

risk_card.data_coverage_note: SCA database contains ~6,982 filings (~35% of estimated universe). NERA/Cornerstone are authoritative full-universe references.

screening_questions.question: Is this a repeat SCA target? How many prior filings, and what were the outcomes?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: 25% of all SCAs involve companies sued before. 46 companies have been sued 4+ times. Prior history is a base-rate multiplier — plaintiffs bar has institutional memory.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No prior SCAs. Or: 1 prior SCA that was dismissed, >5 years ago.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any prior settled SCA. 2+ prior filings regardless of outcome. Active ongoing SCA.

screening_questions.question: Has the company experienced a significant operational disruption (supply chain, manufacturing, IT outage, data breach) in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Operational failures have 61% dismissal rate — better odds for defense — but when they settle, avg is $55M. The CrowdStrike case is the archetype.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No material operational disruptions. Diversified supply chain. Demonstrated business continuity capabilities.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Major disruption disclosed (plant shutdown, supply chain break, IT system failure, product recall). Customer concentration means one disruption hits hard.

screening_questions.question: Has the stock experienced a >15% single-day drop in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Stock drops >15% are the mechanical trigger for plaintiffs firms to evaluate filing. Larger drops = larger damages = higher settlement.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No single-day drop >10% in 12 months. Low volatility stock.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any single-day drop >15%. Multiple drops >10%. Stock down >30% from 12-month high.

screening_questions.question: Does the company have customer concentration >20% in any single customer?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Customer concentration amplifies operational failure risk — losing one customer due to operational problems creates both the revenue miss and the stock drop.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No customer >10% of revenue. Diversified customer base across industries.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any customer >20% of revenue. Government contract dependency. Key platform dependency (e.g., single cloud provider).

screening_questions.question: What is short interest as a % of float? Has it increased >50% in the last 90 days?
screening_questions.data_source: FINRA short interest data, short seller report databases
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Rising short interest is a leading indicator of both stock drops and short-seller reports that trigger SCAs (Hercules Capital/HTGC pattern).
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Short interest <3% of float, stable or declining.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Short interest >8% of float, or any increase >50% in 90 days. Named in short seller report.

screening_questions.question: What is the market cap? How does it map to the settlement severity curve for the relevant scenario?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Market cap is the strongest predictor of settlement size — its the damages denominator. A $50B company with a 20% drop has a $10B damages pool.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Market cap <$1B with simple business model — severity likely <$10M even if case settles.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Market cap >$10B — enters the zone where P90 settlements exceed $100M. Tower adequacy becomes critical.
